# Market Intelligence — Review, Tie-Backs & Demo Script

*Screen labels: MI1 \= Inbox/signal list, MI2 \= Log-intelligence form (top), MI3 \= Log form (bottom), MI4 \= Signal detail drawer.*

---

MI1

## ![][image1]

MI2

## 

## 

## PART A — Quick read

This is your strongest-built Intelligence module. The capture form (MI2/MI3), the triage inbox (MI1), and the **"Agent model instruction"** panel (MI4) already tell the story: fragmented field intel → structured signal → fed to the model. The single biggest opportunity isn't a new screen — it's **making the tie-back visible.** Right now "Apply to model" is a black box; the moment you *show* what it changes downstream, this module goes from "suggestion box" to "the field steering the buy." Part C is how.

**One cross-module catch and one in-module conflict to fix first:**

- **SKU-10198 is used two contradictory ways.** It's the poster child for the **Barnwood Oak returns *threat*** ("hold pending QA") *and* it's an **affected SKU on the Ponce City development *opportunity*** (MI1/MI4 — "upgrade confidence"). You can't tell the model both "hold it" and "uplift it." Use a different SKU for one of them.  
- **Cluster name drift.** MI4 calls the Atlanta cluster **"Southeast Suburban"**; clustering and Peer Intelligence call the same Atlanta stores **"Pro-Heavy South."** Pick one name everywhere.

---

## PART B — Screen-by-screen improvements

### MI1 — Inbox

- **Rename "FEED MODEL 5."** It's the most important KPI and the most ambiguous. Make it **"Applied to model (5)"** and color the strip semantically — threat red, opportunity green, awaiting-review amber.  
- **Put confidence on every card.** Your whole pitch is "filter the noise," but confidence (Anecdotal / Multiple / Confirmed) only appears in the form and detail — not on the inbox cards. Add a confidence badge per card, let users **sort/filter by it, and visually demote anecdotal.** That's "filter the noise," made visible.  
- **Add the ROI KPI:** "**Signals that changed a decision**" (this season). That single number is the answer to "is logging worth my team's time?"  
- **Make "Apply to model" open the instruction first.** A one-click "apply to model" is a black box; route it through the MI4 instruction preview so the user sees exactly what will change before committing.  
- **Label the robot icon** (agent-suggested tags) — right now it's an unexplained glyph.  
- **Keep the contextual actions** — gap signals offer "Line Gap / Field Request," others offer "Apply to model." That routing is smart; just make it legible (a tiny "routes to Range Build" hint on gap actions).

### MI2 / MI3 — Log-intelligence form

- **Let Ask Alan pre-fill the tags from the free text.** Today the *human* picks type / direction / scope / SKUs and the prose "stays human-read only." You described "extract the right signal" — so flip it: the user types what they saw, **the agent proposes the structure, the human confirms.** That's the agentic capture, and it's a much lower-friction ask for a busy store associate.

### MI4 — Signal detail (your best screen — make it the hero)

- **Add a "downstream impact preview."** This is the highest-value change in the module. Next to **"Apply structured tags to model,"** show the chain it will trigger: *"↑ forecast for SKU-11020 in ATL cluster (FW25 / SS26) · raise Peer-Intelligence confidence · surface in next PLR range build."* That single panel turns the abstract "apply to model" into a visible cause-and-effect — and it's exactly what tomorrow's room will ask for.  
- **Show why this signal is trusted:** author **Regional VP** \+ **confirmed** \= high weight. Make the role→confidence weighting explicit.  
- The **"Agent model instruction"** box (structured) vs the free-text observation (human-only) separation is great — keep it; just label which is which.

### KPIs — show / drop

- **Show:** total signals · awaiting review · threat-vs-opportunity split · **confidence mix** · applied-to-model · **signals→decisions (ROI)**.  
- **Drop/rename:** the ambiguous "Feed model."

---

## PART C — Where a signal goes (the tie-back — your core question)

**The mechanism, plainly:** a signal never silently overrides the math. It does three things — (1) **adjusts a SKU's confidence**, (2) **uplifts or dampens its forecast** for a scope \+ season, (3) **injects a tag or a line-gap** the planner sees. Then the model re-recommends with that input. Human knowledge in, explainable recommendation out.

**The connective tissue you already have:** remember the Peer-Intelligence confidence breakdown had a component called **"Annotation overrides"**? *That is the market-intelligence hook.* A logged signal **is** the annotation that moves a SKU's confidence in Peer Intelligence. Wire those two screens together and the loop is literally visible — rename it "Field-intel overrides" so the connection reads.

**Which signal type flows where:**

| Signal type | Direction | Primarily feeds | What it does |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Market — e.g. new development | Opportunity | **Forecast → PLR** | demand uplift → carry \+ depth in scoped stores |
| Product quality — e.g. returns | Threat | **Peer / Hindsight → PLR** | confidence ↓, de-winner, "hold — don't expand" |
| Customer / contractor — a gap | Opportunity | **Range Build** | becomes a line-gap / field-wishlist SKU \+ like-item forecast |
| Competitive — competitor move | Threat | **Peer / Pricing** | watch / defend flag, confidence ↓ |
| Supply chain — lead times | Threat | **Forecast / Hindsight** | dampen forecast; annotate history so a stockout dip isn't misread as weak demand |
| Trend — emerging demand | Opportunity | **Forecast → Range Build** | early uplift, new-range candidate |

So the answer to "forecasting? hindsight? peer?" is **all of them** — by type. Forecasting for demand-drivers, Peer/Hindsight for classification and reading history correctly, Range Build for gaps.

### The hero example — trace it end to end (use this in the demo)

**"Ponce City South — 800 units" (Jason R., Regional VP · confirmed).**

1. **Capture** — a Regional VP logs a confirmed opportunity: an 800-unit mixed-use development breaking ground 3 miles from the Midtown and Buckhead stores; large-format tile and LVP will lead. *No sales history could ever contain this — it hasn't happened yet.*  
2. **Structure** — Ask Alan turns it into an instruction: *upgrade confidence for large-format tile & LVP SKUs in the ATL cluster for FW25 & SS26; tag "development signal: Ponce City South."*  
3. **Forecast** — those SKUs get a demand uplift in that cluster for those seasons. The forecast now sees demand the history can't.  
4. **Peer Intelligence** — the affected SKUs' confidence rises (the "annotation override" component), so they're more likely classified as winners to carry.  
5. **PLR / Curation** — the next Atlanta-cluster PLR recommends **carrying additional large-format porcelain and buying deeper** in Midtown and Buckhead — with the reason "Ponce City South" attached to the line.  
6. **NPI** — the incremental carry \+ depth carries an estimated revenue figure. The loop closes: *the field intel has a dollar value.*

**The line to say:** *"A Regional VP's knowledge of a construction project — something no algorithm could know — became a deeper, smarter buy in three specific stores, with a dollar attached. That's human plus agent."*

### Two supporting examples (in the doc, optional in the demo)

- **Barnwood Oak returns up 22% (threat).** Sell-through looks fine, so the data alone would say "expand." The signal **lowers confidence and flags HOLD** → the next PLR does *not* roll it to more stores pending QA. *The field stops you amplifying a quality problem across the network.*  
- **24×48 rectified porcelain — Pro asked, not in catalogue (gap).** An $80k/yr Pro's request becomes a **line-gap in the next Range Build**, with like-item demand transferred off the named comps (Bianco Lasa / Gris). *This is literally where the "field wishlist" in your PLR comes from.*
